intro.title: How to Use This Worksheet
intro.body: This worksheet covers everything you need for the Module 5 Exam—plus additional Security+ concepts that will serve you well beyond this course. If you work through this honestly, you’ll be prepared. If you skip it, you’re gambling. Your call.

sections.title: Risk Management Fundamentals

content.text: Define each term. If you can’t explain it simply, you don’t understand it well enough.

items: Risk — possibility of suffering harm or loss
Threat — potential cause of unwanted incident
Vulnerability — weakness that can be exploited
Asset — anything of value to organization
Impact — actual damage when threat exploits vulnerability
Likelihood — probability threat will exploit vulnerability
Attack vector — path attacker uses to reach target
Threat vector — method threat uses to gain access
Risk cannot be completely eliminated — it’s absolute
Not every risk needs to be mitigated
Risk management must balance cost vs. benefit
Qualitative and quantitative approaches both have value

sections.subtitle: Threat, vulnerability, risk, and how they relate

sections.title: Quantitative Risk Analysis

items: SLE (Single Loss Expectancy) = AV × EF
ALE (Annualized Loss Expectancy) = SLE × ARO
AV = Asset Value
EF = Exposure Factor (percentage of asset lost)
ARO = Annualized Rate of Occurrence
TCO (Total Cost of Ownership) — all costs over asset lifetime
ROI (Return on Investment) — benefit vs. cost ratio
MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) — average time before failure
MTTR (Mean Time to Repair) — average time to restore
RTO (Recovery Time Objective) — maximum acceptable downtime
RPO (Recovery Point Objective) — maximum acceptable data loss

content.prompt:
A server farm has 5 servers. If 2 fail in an incident, what is the Exposure Factor?
Answer: ___

sections.subtitle: ALE, SLE, ARO, and the math that drives risk decisions

items: Uses professional judgment and experience
Categorizes risks as High/Medium/Low
Risk matrix — plots likelihood vs. impact
5×5 matrix — more granular than 3×3
Faster than quantitative, less precise
Subjective but valuable for prioritization
Risk register — documented list of identified risks
Risk owner — person accountable for managing specific risk
Risk appetite — level of risk organization willing to accept
Risk tolerance — acceptable deviation from risk appetite
Residual risk — risk remaining after controls implemented
Inherent risk — risk before any controls

sections.title: Risk Treatment Strategies

items: Mitigate/Reduce — implement controls to reduce likelihood or impact
Transfer — shift risk to third party (insurance, outsourcing)
Accept — acknowledge risk and continue without action
Avoid — eliminate risk by discontinuing activity
Cost of control should not exceed potential loss
Accept when control costs exceed risk impact
Transfer when risk is outside core competency
Avoid when risk is unacceptable and no reasonable control exists
Cyber insurance — example of risk transfer

sections.subtitle: Mitigate, transfer, accept, avoid — and when each applies

sections.title: Vulnerability Management

items: CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures) — standardized IDs
NVD (National Vulnerability Database) — NIST-maintained database
Exploit-DB — public exploit database
Vendor advisories — Microsoft, Cisco, etc.
Bug bounty programs — crowdsourced vulnerability discovery
Version 3.1 is current standard
Scores range 0.0 to 10.0
None: 0.0 • Low: 0.1–3.9 • Medium: 4.0–6.9 • High: 7.0–8.9 • Critical: 9.0–10.0
Credentialed scan — authenticates to target, more thorough
Non-credentialed scan — external perspective only
Active scanning — sends probes to target
Passive scanning — observes traffic without probing
False positive — reported vulnerability that doesn’t exist
False negative — missed actual vulnerability

sections.subtitle: CVE, CVSS, and the scan-and-remediate cycle

sections.title: Infrastructure Security

items: Firewall — implicit deny, rule-based filtering
Next-gen firewall (NGFW) — application awareness, IPS, deep inspection
WAF (Web Application Firewall) — protects web apps
UTM (Unified Threat Management) — multiple security functions
NAC (Network Access Control) — verifies device compliance
DLP (Data Loss Prevention) — prevents unauthorized data transfer
Encryption at rest — stored data encrypted
Encryption in transit — data encrypted during transmission
Data classification — categorizing data by sensitivity
Data masking — obscuring sensitive data
Tokenization — replacing sensitive data with tokens
Hypervisor — Type 1 (bare metal) vs. Type 2 (hosted)
VM escape — breaking out of VM to host
VM sprawl — uncontrolled VM proliferation
Snapshot — point-in-time VM state
Resource isolation — preventing VMs from affecting each other
ARP poisoning — falsifying ARP responses
MAC flooding — overwhelming switch CAM table
VLAN hopping — gaining access to other VLANs
DNS poisoning — corrupting DNS cache

sections.subtitle: Network devices, data protection, and virtualization defenses

sections.title: Lab Knowledge — Risk Assessment

Answer: ___

Answer: ___

Answer: ___

sections.subtitle: CVSS scoring practice and risk-matrix construction

sections.title: Professional Application

content.text: Ethics, communication, and judgment matter as much as technical skills.

items: Your vulnerability scan shows 500+ findings. How do you prioritize what the client should fix first?
A client asks why they should pay for cyber insurance when they already have security controls. How do you explain?
Leadership wants to know the ROI of a proposed security investment. What metrics would you use?
A critical vulnerability exists in a system that cannot be patched due to vendor support issues. What risk treatment options exist?
You find an unpatched Apache server with a known exploit. Red team: what’s your attack path? Blue team: what’s the remediation?
A client has no vulnerability management program. Red team: why is this organization an easy target? Blue team: what process do you recommend?

sections.subtitle: Communicating risk to executives and engineers alike

content.text: If you can answer these, you understand the material. If not, go back and review.

items: Explain the relationship between threat, vulnerability, and risk.
Calculate ALE given: Asset Value = $100,000, Exposure Factor = 40%, ARO = 2
When should an organization choose to accept a risk rather than mitigate it?
What’s the difference between quantitative and qualitative risk analysis?
Explain what residual risk is and why it matters.
What is the purpose of CVSS scoring?
Why might a CVSS 6.5 vulnerability be fixed before a CVSS 9.0?
What are the four risk treatment strategies?
What’s the difference between a SYN scan and a TCP connect scan?
Why is business context important when prioritizing vulnerabilities?

closing.body: That’s everything. If you worked through this worksheet honestly—defining terms, answering questions, recording your lab results—you’re prepared for the Module 5 Exam and you’ve built a foundation for Security+. If you skimmed it or skipped it, that’s your choice. The exam will reveal which group you’re in.
